reasoning_model_1: The model successfully generates the speech content 'Mm, this dish tastes really good!' with accurate pronunciation and good fluency. However, regarding the core paralinguistic requirement, the model fails to explicitly generate a 'smack of the lips'. The audio begins immediately with the voiced hum 'Mm' at 00:00 without any preceding percussive lip-smacking sound. While the 'Mm' itself is tonal, it functions more as a reading of the text rather than a non-linguistic vocalization effect added to the speech. The specific instruction to include the physical sound of a lip smack is not audible.
reasoning_model_2: The model accurately generates the text content. Crucially, the model adheres to the paralinguistic instruction by including a distinct non-linguistic sound event at the very beginning of the audio (00:00-00:01). Before the verbal 'Mm' begins at 00:01, there is an audible mouth sound/smack that simulates the action of tasting or anticipating food. This addition makes the subsequent 'Mm, this dish tastes really good' feel more natural and contextually appropriate. The integration of the sound effect is smooth and does not disrupt the fluency of the speech.
model_comparison: Both models deliver the spoken text accurately with good naturalness. The decisive factor is the 'Feature Control' for the requested 'smack of the lips'. Model 1 (T1) ignores the request for the specific sound effect and simply reads the text 'Mm' with a standard intonation. Model 2 (T2) successfully interprets the command, inserting a clear non-linguistic mouth sound (the smack) in the second before speaking the text. This demonstrates superior control over paralinguistic features and a better understanding of the prompt's instructional constraints.
